Biosphere 2

Context + Hook Everyone knows Earth is facing an unprecedented crisis. Climate change has already locked Earth on a +2°C warming by 2050. There are many threats to the race of humanity that could render the entire race extinct. Such as resource wars over basic materials such as water, rare earths, and arable land, or there could be an asteroid impact that sends us to the same situation as the dinosaurs, rendering us to extinction. There are much more threats on Earth itself that could already extinct the human species. These all tells us humans should not depend on Earth alone. Physicist Stephen Hawking warned in 2017 that "We face awesome dangers, but it may be possible to avoid them if we choose. Our only hope lies in spreading humanity across our solar system." Then what may save our species called homo sapiens from extinction, you may ask. What can ensure our future generations, our culture, and most importantly the knowledge we humans have gained throughout hundreds of years not go to waste? Well the solution is easy, as Stephen Hawking has said, we could become a multi-planet species. Well but which planet will be the most suitable for us humans to live on? The earth similarity index (ESI), which is a scale from 0 to 1 (1 being an exact Earth twin) used to quantify the physical similarity of planets or moons to Earth could be a valid way to see which planet suits us most. According to NASA, Mars’ score is 0.735. It is both similar and close to earth. Among all possible destinations, Mars stands out as the most realistic candidate for our second earth. Although the current environment in Mars is in no terms, habitable, Mars is not an empty rock, it once had flowing water, a thicker atmosphere, and environmental conditions that may have been suitable for life. MMars, however, llost much of its atmosphere and its global magnetic field. Making it exposed to solar wind and harsh radiation throughout space. There is no breathable atmosphere, no natural shielding against cosmic radiation, and certainly no stable surface ecosystem that can support human life. Then what can make it possible for humanity to ever live there? The answer to the question is that it must create a protected environment from the ground up. But terraforming mars isn't really realistic, it takes time, effort and a huge amount of money. The more realistically possible way is humans building a sealed, self-sustaining habitat that can support life on mars using controlled ecological systems. An easier to understand way of saying it, is to create a small yet stable biosphere that could inhabit us humans on mars. This is where the idea of a closed ecological system comes into play. If we can't make Mars support human life naturally, then humans need to build the conditions for life in a small area. Here is where Mars is important for our mission, even though it is practically a wasteland there, it had and still has most of the basic materials needed to sustain life, such as water, oxygen, carbon dioxide, all trapped inside rocks or underground. Unlike planets like Jupiter which is a gas giant that doesn't even have a land surface, or Mercury, which is tidally locked on the Sun, so either facing the hot Sun, reaching unbearably high temperatures, or the opposite side, which is about -180 Celcius. BIOSPHERRE 2 : NIINTORODUCTION odsphere 2 was built in the Arizona desert in the early 1990s as a massive sealed ecosystem experiment. Well you might be wondering why it matters, it is because it is an experiment conducted on Earth that could turn our dreams into reality. Before it, people weren't sure about whether a sealed human habitat was even possible; but after it, the answer became “yes”, and it also demonstrates clearly what possible problems we may encounter if we really were to build a biosphere on Mars. Now, allow me to explain what happened in biosphere 2 briefly. Biosphere 2 was a project launched in 1984 by businessman and billionaire philanthropist Ed Bass and systems ecologist John P. Allen. Bass provided USD150 million in funding until 1991. It was named “Biosphere 2” because it was meant to be the second fully self-sufficient biosphere, after the Earth itself. Now enough with background introduction, its engineering design basically aims for creating a closed system, where energy could enter and leave, while matter cannot. Trying to imitate Mars’ condition where there is still sunlight yet there are no materials that could directly support life. One of the innovations it had was the patented airtight sealing methods, developed by Pearce and William Dempster, achieved a leak rate of less than 10% per year, which is nothing short of impressive. It has many more systems where we will go through it in our problems and solution parts later on. To put it simply in a nutshell, it is an airtight glass structure with seven distinct biomes under a sealed envelope. Starting from September 26,1991, eight humans lived inside for 730 consecutive days. The longest close human life support mission ever conducted in human history. With no material exchange except sunlight and information, mostly mimicking the environment on Mars. SUCCESStHE PThe project proved and demonstrated some successes or what is viable, while also showing what fails or falls short. We’ll talk about the successes first. First, the biosphere 2 successfully established self-sustaining populations across seven biomes, with over 3800 species. Biomes include rainforest, savanna, ocean with coral reefs, desert, intensive agriculture, human habitat, and marsh terrains. We believe with more funding, it could contain more diversity and have a closer resemblance to the real ecosystem we have on our mother Earth. Second is the food production. The 2000 square metres agriculture biome initially provided about 80% of crew caloric needs by bananas, papayas, sweet potatoes, peanuts, beans, rice and cotton and much more. Yields in the second mission even exceeded Earth’s agriculture despite it only having 40-50% light levels, also similar to mars. And most importantly no insect pollinators. The crew’s other 20% calories need comes from animal protein from about 8 tilapia, 4 goats, 35 chickens, and 6 pigs, even though there were frequent pest outbreaks. With more human resources to maintain the agriculture and livestock, we can definitely reach a new height. Third is that the water cycled 100% internally. About 1.1 million liters of initial water recirculated by 3km pipes through evapotranspiration, constructed wetlands, soil filtration and condensation onto about 4000 square metre glass. About a daily collection of ten thousand liters matching evapotranspiration. Treating human and animal waste drained about 0.66 meters cube per week without measurable loss. Nitrogen cycled through legume fixation, composting, and microbial activity in about 6000m cube soil volume. It maintained fertility without any synthetic fertilizers, also an impressive feat. As for carbon, it cycled via photosynthesis balancing respiration, while the seven lung-like “technospheres” helped with stabilizing the pressure at right about 101kPa despite huge temperature swings from -2 degrees celsius to 40 degrees celsius. Fourth, in the second experiment of biosphere 2. Bees were introduced. Domestic species thrived there, with bees pollinated 80% crops, chickens laid over 2500 eggs, goats produced abut 450 liters of milk, tilapia grew 300% biomass. 80% food closure,100% water recycling, 3800 species equilibrium ( well not exactly, will be explained in problems and solutions ), zero external mass input except information or sunlight which both could also be acquired on mars so there wont be anything different. These all proved at least the mere possibility that human can build a small ecosystem in Earth biospheres capable of multi-year operations. Thanks to this experiment, it became more apparent that this dream of humanity could be nurtured into reality. wesbsitesSTUFF TO IMPROVE/JAMES’ STUFF tufFirst problem would be the solar wind. So what is a solar wind? The solar wind is a flow of particles that comes off the sun at about one million miles per hour and travels throughout the entire solar system. First proposed in the 1950s by University of Chicago physicist Eugene Parker, the solar wind is visible in the halo around the sun during an eclipse and sometimes when the particles hit the Earth’s atmosphere—as the aurora borealis, or northern lights. So how does it relate to Mars then? And how would it possibly affect our biosphere on Mars? Solar wind is very destructive, it was the reason why Mars doesn’t have an atmosphere today. It poses a severe, chronic radiation hazard to humans on Mars because the planet lacks a global magnetic field, and only has a thin atmosphere which offers little to none protection. These could cause potential humans living on Mars to have acute radiation sickness, long-term cancer risks, and cognitive damage. Earth is most fortunate to have vast webs of magnetic fields surrounding it. Without them, our atmosphere is mostly likely getting torn away by solar winds and we won’t be existing on our planet Earth. So establishing a magnetic field is one of many important steps to build a biosphere on Mars. We can’t possibly heat up Mar’s core to start the dynamo like Earth, so scientists have already hypothesized several ways of protecting the planet using an artificial magnetic field. One method could be building a superconductor ring between Mars and the Sun like a shield or umbrella to protect the planet from those high-energy solar particles. * aA diagram made by NASA, which we could use to visualize the whole process. The shield structure would consist of a large dipole— a closed electric circuit powerful enough to generate an artificial magnetic field. It would be put between Mars and the Sun, about the place where it is called a Lagrange point. In a nutshell, it is basically gravitational equilibrium points between two massive bodies in space, so it wouldn’t just fall into either side.siAnother idea from a 2021/2022 paper by Ruth A. Bamford et al titled “How to create an artificial magnetosphere for Mars” theorized that we can create an artificial charged particle ring around Mars by ionizing or ejecting materials from Phobos, using electromagnetic waves to drive the current through the whole planet. So a superconductor belt around the whole planet to create an artificial magnetic field for Mars. Then which is better or which is worse? From our point of view, we think both have their own good and bad sides. Both artificial magnetic fields don't need to be too strong. Scientists estimated that Mars’ magnetic field would only need to be around 1/40 as strong as Earth’s. But both ways would need to be made out of rare earth materials, which would be very difficult to find on Mars. And it would cost a lot of money to transport them to Mars from Earth. So which one requires less materials? Well, if you think about it, you might have thought a small satellite won't need too much materials. But the satellite would need to be the size of a space station, and it is already hard to launch a satellite from Earth. Launching a satellite from Mars would be literally impossible. Ironically, the belt might require less materials to build. Well an actual superconductor ring must create a field strong enough to protect Mars, but not as strong to damage itself. And according to Brilliant, it turns out that practical magnetic field constraints lead to wire thickness being proportional to the 1/ the loop radius squared in an optimized ring design. So a small loop radius would be accompanied by a thick wire radius and the same for the opposite. So for the belt, we will need to span the circumference of Mars, it must have a large loop radius and a thin wire radius. While the umbrella would need a smaller loop radius and a thick wire radius. So using the torus model, we can see that a large loop radius and thin wire results in a smaller volume. Because the volume increases greatly when wire thickness is increased. The volume of torus  V=2𝛑2w2L, is dependent on w and L, so it can be approximated as V to w squared times L. Then a practical magnetic field constraints lead to w being proportional to 1/L squared in an optimized ring design. We can substitute 1/L squared to w to get V proportional to 1/L cube. So a larger loop radius results in a smaller volume. The belt will have a much larger loop radius but still a smaller volume then the umbrella method. And a larger loop radius means thinner wire radius as well. alSo we can hence know that belt might be the most viable option when it comes to creating an artificial magnetic field for Mars.ly Bsources https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biosphere_2https://biosphere2.org/research/biosphere-2-ecosystemshttps://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/0273117795008618https://www.biospherefoundation.org/pdfs/BIO2accomplishments.pdf https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0925857498000925https://news.uchicago.edu/explainer/what-is-solar-windhttps://astrobiology.nasa.gov/news/how-to-give-mars-anatmosphere-maybe/**https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/0https://brilliant.org/courses/nutshell/576521005099https://astrobiology.com/2021/11/how-to-create-an-artificialmagnetosphere-for-mars.html
